title: What affects the rate of photosynthesis?
examTip: AQA regularly asks you to interpret rate-of-photosynthesis graphs or explain why the rate stops rising. Always name the specific limiting factor and explain what it is doing at the molecular level — don't just say 'it runs out'.
introTips: This lesson is about why photosynthesis speeds up or slows down depending on conditions.
You'll learn the three main limiting factors and how each one affects the rate — including why graphs always plateau.
The inverse square law for light is a favourite AQA calculation — we'll cover exactly how to use it.
intro: A plant sitting in a dim corner isn't photosynthesising slowly because it's lazy — it's being held back. Something in the environment is acting as a brake. Understanding what that brake is, and how to release it, is the whole point of this topic. It's also the science behind why commercial greenhouses use artificial lighting, CO₂ enrichment and temperature control to grow more food.

keyPoints.heading: What a limiting factor actually means
keyPoints.content: The rate of photosynthesis is controlled by whichever essential resource is in shortest supply. That resource is the limiting factor — the one single thing that, if you increased it, would make the rate go up. Think of it like a factory production line: you can have 100 workers and 100 machines, but if there are only 10 raw materials arriving, the output is 10 units. The raw materials are the limiting factor. Remove that limit, and output rises — until something else becomes the new limit. In photosynthesis, the three main limiting factors are light intensity, CO₂ concentration and temperature. Only one is limiting at any given moment.
writeBullets: Limiting factor = the factor in shortest supply that determines the rate of photosynthesis
Increase the limiting factor → rate rises
Rate levels off when another factor becomes the new limit
Only one factor limits the rate at any one time
Three main limiting factors: light intensity, CO₂ concentration, temperature

keyTerms.def: The factor present in shortest supply that determines the rate of a process — increasing it will increase the rate
How fast a plant is converting light energy, CO₂ and water into glucose and oxygen

keyPoints.memTip: Imagine a three-legged stool: the shortest leg determines how level it sits. Lengthen that leg and the stool rises — until another leg becomes the shortest. That's limiting factors. One leg limits everything at any given moment.

keyPoints.heading: Light intensity — and the inverse square law
keyPoints.content: More light means more energy for the light-dependent reactions, so the rate of photosynthesis increases. But on a graph, the rate rises and then plateaus — it levels off even as you keep increasing the light. That's because something else (usually CO₂ concentration or temperature) has become the new limiting factor. The relationship between distance and light intensity follows the inverse square law: if you double the distance between a plant and a lamp, the light intensity drops to one quarter. The formula is: intensity ∝ 1 ÷ distance². So at 20 cm, if you move to 40 cm, the intensity becomes 1/4 of what it was. This is why AQA investigations ask you to measure distance carefully — a small change in distance has a big effect.
writeBullets: More light → faster rate, up to a plateau
Plateau = another factor has become limiting (CO₂ or temperature)
Inverse square law: intensity ∝ 1 ÷ d²
Double distance → intensity becomes ¼ (not ½)
Used in pond-weed experiments: count bubbles of O₂ at different lamp distances

keyTerms.def: The amount of light energy hitting a surface per unit area — greater light intensity means more energy available for photosynthesis
The rule that light intensity is proportional to 1 ÷ distance²: doubling the distance reduces intensity to one quarter
The flat section on a graph where rate stops increasing despite more of one factor being added — another factor is now limiting

keyPoints.memTip: Double the distance, quarter the intensity — the 'square' in inverse square law means the effect is much bigger than you'd expect. If you only remember one thing about the maths here, it's: 2× distance = 4× dimmer, not 2× dimmer.

keyPoints.heading: CO₂ concentration
keyPoints.content: Carbon dioxide is the raw material for the Calvin cycle (the light-independent reactions). More CO₂ means more molecules available to be fixed into glucose, so the rate rises. In a greenhouse, growers deliberately pump in extra CO₂ — sometimes up to three times atmospheric concentration — to push the rate higher. Again, if you graph CO₂ concentration against rate, you see a rise then a plateau. At high CO₂, light intensity or temperature becomes the new limiting factor. Normal atmospheric CO₂ is about 0.04% (400 ppm) — very low — which is why CO₂ is often the limiting factor in natural conditions.
writeBullets: CO₂ is the carbon source for making glucose — more CO₂ = faster rate
Graph: rate rises with CO₂ concentration, then plateaus
Plateau = light or temperature is now limiting
Greenhouses pump in extra CO₂ to increase yield
Normal air is only ~0.04% CO₂ — often the limiting factor outdoors

keyTerms.def: The proportion of carbon dioxide in the air surrounding a plant — a key raw material for photosynthesis
The light-independent reactions in the stroma of the chloroplast where CO₂ is fixed into glucose (AQA Higher context)

keyPoints.memTip: CO₂ is the carbon source — no carbon, no glucose. Greenhouses pump it in because outside air is so CO₂-poor that it's constantly holding plants back. Think: if you want more glucose, give the plant more carbon.

keyPoints.heading: Temperature — enzymes are in control
keyPoints.content: Temperature affects photosynthesis because the reactions are controlled by enzymes. As temperature rises, enzyme activity increases — molecules collide more frequently and with more energy — so the rate of photosynthesis goes up. The rate peaks at an optimum temperature, which is around 25–35 °C for most plants. Above the optimum, the enzymes begin to denature: the active site changes shape, substrates can no longer bind, and the rate falls sharply — not gradually. This is different from the CO₂ and light graphs, which plateau at the top. A temperature graph rises, peaks, then drops. Below the optimum, low temperature simply means slower enzyme activity — the enzymes aren't denatured, just slow.
writeBullets: Temperature ↑ → enzyme activity ↑ → rate of photosynthesis ↑
Optimum temperature ~25–35 °C for most plants
Above optimum: enzymes denature — active site changes shape — rate drops sharply
Below optimum: enzymes are slow but not denatured — rate is low but recoverable
Temperature graph shape: rises, peaks, then falls — NOT a plateau

keyTerms.def: The temperature at which enzyme activity (and therefore reaction rate) is at its highest
Permanent change in the shape of an enzyme's active site due to high temperature (or extreme pH), making it non-functional
The specific region of an enzyme where the substrate binds — its shape must match the substrate exactly

keyPoints.memTip: Temperature graphs for photosynthesis look like a hill — up then back down. CO₂ and light graphs look like a ramp that levels off. The difference is denaturation: enzymes don't just slow down at high temperature, they break. That's why the line drops, not plateaus.

tables.title: Three limiting factors compared
rows: More light → faster rate up to a plateau; inverse square law applies (double distance = ¼ intensity)
Pond-weed experiment: count O₂ bubbles per minute at different lamp distances; keep CO₂ and temperature constant
More CO₂ → faster rate up to a plateau; normal air is only ~0.04% CO₂
Add different concentrations of sodium hydrogencarbonate to the water; measure bubble rate; keep light and temperature constant
Rate rises to optimum (~25–35 °C) then falls sharply as enzymes denature above optimum
Use a water bath to control temperature; measure O₂ production or gas volume at each temperature; keep light and CO₂ constant

1.question: A plant is photosynthesising. You increase the light intensity but the rate doesn't change. What does this tell you?
options: Something else — CO₂ or temperature — is the limiting factor
The plant has too much light and is being damaged
1.correctFeedback: Exactly right. If increasing light doesn't increase the rate, light is NOT the limiting factor. Something else is holding the rate back — most likely CO₂ concentration or temperature.
1.wrongFeedback: If light were the limiting factor, adding more light would increase the rate. Since it doesn't, something else is the bottleneck — probably CO₂ or temperature. The plant is still photosynthesising, just limited by a different factor.

2.question: A lamp is 10 cm from a plant. You move it to 20 cm. What happens to the light intensity received by the plant?
2.correctFeedback: Correct — the inverse square law says intensity ∝ 1 ÷ d². Doubling the distance (10 → 20 cm) means 1 ÷ 2² = 1 ÷ 4. The intensity drops to one quarter.
2.wrongFeedback: The inverse square law is the key here: intensity ∝ 1 ÷ distance². Doubling the distance means the divisor goes from 1² to 2² — so the intensity drops to 1/4, not 1/2. The 'square' makes it a much bigger drop than you might expect.

commonMistakes: Saying 'the rate increases then stays the same because the plant has enough light' — the plateau happens because ANOTHER factor has become limiting, not because the plant is 'satisfied' with the light it has.

Confusing denaturation with inhibition at low temperatures — at low temperature, enzymes work slowly but are not denatured. Denaturation is a permanent change caused by HIGH temperature (or extreme pH).
Using the inverse square law incorrectly and saying doubling the distance halves the intensity — it quarters it. Always square the distance ratio.
Drawing the temperature graph as a plateau rather than a peak-and-drop — CO₂ and light graphs plateau; the temperature graph must show a rise then a sharp fall after the optimum.

Forgetting to name which other factor becomes limiting when the rate plateaus — AQA mark schemes want you to say 'another factor such as CO₂ concentration is now limiting', not just 'something else is limiting it'.
examTips: When describing a rate-of-photosynthesis graph, always describe it in three parts: what happens as the variable increases, where it plateaus, and why it plateaus — naming the new limiting factor.
For the inverse square law calculation, write out intensity ∝ 1/d² first, then substitute — examiners award method marks even if you make an arithmetic error.

If a question asks you to evaluate a graph showing rate against temperature, make sure to note that the drop after the optimum is due to enzyme denaturation, not enzyme inhibition — these score differently.

revisionCardBullets: Limiting factor = factor in shortest supply that controls the rate — increase it and the rate rises
Three main limiting factors: light intensity, CO₂ concentration, temperature
Rate graph plateaus when a DIFFERENT factor becomes limiting — not because there's 'enough'
Inverse square law: intensity ∝ 1 ÷ d² — double distance = ¼ intensity (not ½)
CO₂ concentration: more CO₂ → faster rate → plateau when light or temperature limits instead
Normal air is only ~0.04% CO₂ — greenhouses pump in extra to boost yield
Temperature: rate rises to optimum (~25–35 °C), then falls sharply — enzymes denature above optimum
Temperature graph = rise, peak, sharp drop. Light/CO₂ graphs = rise then plateau
Denaturation = permanent change to active site shape — not reversible (unlike low-temp slowing)
